content.text: copies every own enumerable property of an object onto a JSX element as individual props. It is not React-specific — it is plain JavaScript object spread applied to JSX attribute lists.

Spreading is most useful in two scenarios: forwarding all props a component receives down to a native element (transparent wrappers), and passing a pre-built configuration object to a component when you know the keys at the call site.

attrs.alt: Three prop-spread patterns: transparent wrapper, spread-then-override, and the danger of spread-before-explicit

content.text: The diagram above shows the three common shapes. The key rule:
. Spreading before explicit attributes lets the component set immutable behaviour; spreading after them lets the caller override.
A transparent wrapper receives all its props and forwards them wholesale to a native element. This is the most common use of spread in design systems.
The pattern scales to any native element. An
the same way, giving callers access to
, and ARIA attributes without any explicit prop definitions.
Pattern 2 — Rest + Spread: Separating Owned from Forwarded Props
Most real components own some props and need to forward the rest. The rest parameter in destructuring (
) collects everything not explicitly named, so you can spread only what the native element should receive.
, the component would have to explicitly list every native attribute it wants to forward (
…) and the prop list would never be complete.
JSX resolves attribute conflicts the same way JavaScript object spread does: the last value for a given key wins.
The safest approach is Scenario A when a prop must be immutable and Pattern 2 (rest-then-spread) otherwise. In general: if your component
control a specific attribute, put that attribute

When Spreading is the Wrong Tool
Prop spreading is powerful but should not be the default. It has real downsides:
A reader cannot tell which props the component accepts without tracing through the spread. Explicit destructuring serves as living documentation.
If you spread onto a native HTML element, React will warn (and browsers will ignore) any non-standard attribute names. Use rest-and-spread carefully: strip component-owned props first.
TypeScript can infer the type of
from the function signature, but blindly spreading an untyped
If the spread object is reconstructed on every render (not memoised), its spread props will always be different references, preventing
Use spreading for transparent wrappers and the rest/spread pattern for explicit pass-through. Avoid wholesale spreading (
with no destructuring) into custom components, because any future prop the child component introduces will silently reach the parent.
TypeScript makes the rest/spread pattern especially clean through the
) utility, which gives you the full prop type of any HTML element, letting you extend it with your own props.
This pattern — extending a native element's type, destructuring owned props, and spreading the rest — is the idiomatic TypeScript way to build low-level design-system components.
One of the most common spread-related tasks is combining the component's base class with one optionally passed by the caller. There are several approaches:
the spread, so the component always controls the final class list even when the caller passes their own.
Forwarding Event Handlers While Keeping Your Own

Sometimes a component needs to intercept an event (for logging, validation, or animation) and also let the caller observe it. Simply spreading overwrites the component's handler with the caller's. The fix is to compose both:
handles the case where the caller passes no
— no need for a separate null check or default.
When you build a list of components from data (tables, menus, form fields), spread makes each item's configuration concise.
Each field object in the data array maps directly to element attributes. Any attribute you add to the config object (e.g.
) automatically reaches the element without touching the component code.
Caller overrides component's locked attr (e.g. type)
Put explicit attr after spread, or destructure and exclude it from rest
React warns: "Unknown prop on DOM element"
Destructure component-specific props before spreading rest
Cannot tell what a component accepts without reading its full body
Use TypeScript types or PropTypes; add JSDoc; name the rest parameter descriptively (e.g. ...buttonProps)
Component's onClick fires but caller's is silently dropped
Compose both handlers in a wrapper function; call onClick?.(e) at the end
Spread doesn't include ref (it's not a real prop)
Use React.forwardRef to explicitly pass ref to the inner element
is not a normal prop. It is handled by React before the component function runs, so it never appears in
. If a consumer needs to attach a DOM ref to the underlying element, the component must use
This applies equally to transparent wrappers. Any low-level design-system input, button, or focusable element should use
so imperative focus management works correctly.
You can spread a conditionally-built object to apply a group of props together:
in JSX is a no-op (React ignores it), which makes the
Spreading itself has no meaningful runtime cost compared to explicit prop passing — React processes both the same way. The performance concern is around object identity:
If you construct the object to spread inline (
), a new object is created every render — but React still diffs individual prop values, not the object, so this has no extra cost over listing each attribute.
If you pass the spread object as a prop to a memoised child (
), the object reference changes every render and memo won't bail out. Here,

Higher-order components (HOCs) — functions that take a component and return an enhanced one — rely on prop spreading to be transparent. Without it, the HOC would have to explicitly forward every prop the wrapped component accepts.
The pattern is the same as in Pattern 2 above: destructure the props the HOC owns (
), collect the rest, then spread those rest props onto the wrapped component. The HOC remains agnostic to which specific props the wrapped component takes.
Before destructuring defaults became standard, React components used a static
would apply before the spread, meaning the caller could override defaults through the spread.
Understanding the legacy pattern is useful when reading older codebases, but new code should use destructuring defaults exclusively.
component demonstrates every spread technique from this section in a realistic design-system implementation.

the spread when the component must control them; before when the caller should win.
to forward it to the underlying element.
) when both the component and the caller need to respond to the same event.
Prefer explicit destructuring over spread-everything: it makes the prop surface visible, aids TypeScript, and prevents unknown-attribute React warnings.
